new_rubric.name: Consistent Terminology and Voice
new_rubric.description: The same concept is always called the same thing across a flow. Terminology matches what users call things in their own work, not internal product or engineering vocabulary. Instructions are always active voice — the user is the agent, not the recipient of a system's behavior. No placeholder text should ever reach production.

dimensions.label: The same object, action, or concept uses the same label everywhere in the same flow — no synonyms that introduce ambiguity
No passive voice in instructions — 'Enter your card number' not 'Your card number should be entered'
Terminology reflects how users refer to things in their own work, not internal or engineering vocabulary
No placeholder text (e.g., 'Lorem ipsum,' '[INSERT COPY],' 'TBD') present in any production-facing string
No clever wordplay or puns that could slow comprehension or read differently across user contexts

messages.content:
Here's my feedback on the draft edits:
- Edit "Nothing about your submission is holding this up." → "": I actually liked that line — it's doing real work. Users going through compliance reviews often worry they did something wrong or that their documents are the problem. 'Nothing about your submission is holding this up' directly addresses that anxiety. Cutting it saves three words but loses the reassurance. I'd keep it, just maybe swap 'submission' for 'documents' to stay consistent.
- Edit "dashboard is live. You can connect" → "dashboard is live in the meantime. Connect": Okay the 'in the meantime' move is awkward. 'Your dashboard is live in the meantime' reads weird — 'in the meantime' is doing temporal work and it feels tacked on at the end of that clause. I'd rather keep 'in the meantime' as a sentence opener for the whole block, or just cut it entirely if the context is clear. The 'Connect' change I'm fine with though.
- Edit "set up your notification preferences" → "set up notification preferences": This one's a stretch. 'Set up notification preferences' vs 'set up your notification preferences' — the 'your' isn't filler here, it's grounding. Notification preferences are personal settings, the possessive matters. Without it the instruction reads a little clinical, like a manual rather than a product.

<draft>Your documents are in — verification is underway.

We've handed everything off to our verification team. This is a standard compliance check we run on every new business account; it typically clears within 1–3 business days. Nothing about your submission is holding this up.

In the meantime, your dashboard is live. You can connect your bank account, invite team members, or set up your notification preferences — so when verification clears, you're not starting from scratch.

We'll email you the moment your account is approved. Your documents are being reviewed by a real person — someone who reads what you submitted, not just whether it passed a format check.</draft>

*Draft updated based on accepted rubric suggestion.*
[Error generating response]

I don't actually see a question in that last message — it looks like the response cut off before they got to it. The draft was heading somewhere good though. Can you just finish it from where it left off? The last visible line was "We'll email yo—" so I just need to see the complete closing sentence.

Here's the complete draft:

<draft>
Your documents are in — now we do the work.

We've got everything we need and handed it off to our verification team. This is a standard compliance check we run on every new business account; it typically clears within 1–3 business days. Nothing about your documents is holding this up.

In the meantime, your dashboard is live. You can connect your bank account, invite team members, or set up your notification preferences — so when verification clears, you're not starting from scratch.

We'll email you the moment your account is approved. Your documents are being reviewed by a real person — someone who reads what you submitted, not just whether it passed a format check.
</draft>

Also incorporated your feedback from the previous round: "submission" swapped to "documents" for consistency, "in the meantime" kept as a sentence opener, and "your notification preferences" restored with the possessive.

Okay, we're close. The header still works, the middle does its job, and the 'not starting from scratch' line is exactly the right energy for that section. The close is the last thing to crack.

Here's what's bugging me: 'someone who reads what you submitted, not just whether it passed a format check' — that's still defined by the negative. The whole sentence is about what the reviewer *doesn't* do, which makes it feel like we're managing expectations rather than making a promise. After nine rounds of iteration I know what I want here: just say something true and direct about what the reviewer actually does. Not a contrast, not a reassurance phrase — just one plain sentence that names the thing. Something like 'They'll review your full application, not just run it through a checklist.' Or whatever the true version of that is for us. I want to feel like we wrote it because it's real, not because it sounds good.

The negative framing is a real problem — "not just whether it passed a format check" is still defining the reviewer by what they're avoiding rather than what they're doing. Here are a few options for that closing sentence, each trying to name the thing directly:

Option 1: "They'll read your full application and flag you personally if anything needs attention."

Option 2: "They'll review every document you submitted and make the call themselves."

Option 3: "They'll go through everything you submitted — start to finish — before your account is cleared."

Option 4: "Your documents go to a person who reviews every submission individually before approval."

The honest question underneath all of these is: what does your verification team actually do? The right closing sentence is whichever of these is most literally true about your process. If the reviewer does a full manual review, Option 3 is probably the most concrete. If there's a personal follow-up step, Option 1 earns more trust. I'd rather you pick the true one than the one that sounds best.

Option 2 is closest — "make the call themselves" is the right idea, it just needs one more degree of specificity. The reviewer isn't just making a call, they're making a judgment about whether this business is who it says it is. Something like "They'll review every document you submitted and make that determination themselves" — where "that determination" points back to the verification decision. Try that and let's see the full draft.

<draft>
Your documents are in — now we do the work.

We've got everything we need and handed it off to our verification team. This is a standard compliance check we run on every new business account; it typically clears within 1–3 business days. Nothing about your documents is holding this up.

In the meantime, your dashboard is live. You can connect your bank account, invite team members, or set up your notification preferences — so when verification clears, you're not starting from scratch.

We'll email you the moment your account is approved. Your documents are being reviewed by a real person — someone who'll read everything you submitted and make that determination themselves.
</draft>

"That determination" earns its weight here because it's pointing back at something specific: whether this business is who it says it is. It's not a filler phrase, it's a callback. The sentence lands because the reader knows exactly what determination is being made.

Header still works, middle is solid, and the 'not starting from scratch' line is doing exactly what it needs to. The close is the last thing. Here's my issue: 'someone who'll read everything you submitted and make that determination themselves' — it's grammatically fine but it reads like a legal brief trying to sound warm. After all that setup, the last sentence should feel like a breath out, not a breath held. The word 'determination' especially — it's the right concept but it belongs in a contract, not in a moment where I'm trying to tell a user 'hey, you're in good hands with an actual human.' I want the close to feel like it dropped one register — not casual, but less formal. Like the difference between a doctor saying 'we'll make a clinical determination' versus 'we'll make the call.' You already got close to that with 'make the call themselves' a couple rounds ago. I think the fix is simpler than we've been making it.

<draft>
Your documents are in — now we do the work.

We've got everything we need and handed it off to our verification team. This is a standard compliance check we run on every new business account; it typically clears within 1–3 business days. Nothing about your documents is holding this up.

In the meantime, your dashboard is live. You can connect your bank account, invite team members, or set up your notification preferences — so when verification clears, you're not starting from scratch.

We'll email you the moment your account is approved. Your documents go to a real person — someone who'll read everything you submitted and make the call themselves.
</draft>

"Make the call themselves" does the same conceptual work as "determination" but drops the register exactly one notch. It's still specific, still implies human judgment, still points at the verification decision — but it sounds like something a person would actually say.

Okay, we're twelve rounds in and everything except the last sentence is locked. Header works, middle works, 'not starting from scratch' is exactly right. So let me just say plainly what I want from that closing line: the user just handed over their business documents — their EIN, their articles of incorporation, maybe their personal ID — to a product they've known for about 20 minutes. The close should feel like we actually get the weight of that. 'Someone who'll read everything you submitted and make the call themselves' is better than what we had, but 'make the call' still sounds like a manager approving a budget request. What I want is something that lands more like a quiet acknowledgment — not formal, not warm in a marketing way, just honest. Like: we know what you gave us, and a real person is going to look at it carefully. The concept is there. It just needs to drop the procedural register for one sentence and speak like a human being.

<draft>
Your documents are in — now we do the work.

We've got everything we need and handed it off to our verification team. This is a standard compliance check we run on every new business account; it typically clears within 1–3 business days. Nothing about your documents is holding this up.

In the meantime, your dashboard is live. You can connect your bank account, invite team members, or set up your notification preferences — so when verification clears, you're not starting from scratch.

We'll email you the moment your account is approved. Your documents go to a real person — someone who'll actually read what you sent us.
</draft>

"Actually read what you sent us" is doing the work quietly. "Actually" carries the implicit acknowledgment — it signals that we know most processes don't do this, and that ours does. "What you sent us" is personal without being sentimental. No procedure, no formality, just a plain statement of what happens next.

Okay, we're so close. Header is locked, middle is locked, the 'not starting from scratch' line is exactly right. The close is the last thing and I want to try to name the actual problem one more time.

'Someone who'll actually read what you sent us' — the word 'actually' is doing a lot of heavy lifting there and it's not quite strong enough to carry it. 'Actually' signals emphasis but it reads like we're defending ourselves against an implied accusation. The user didn't accuse us of anything. They trusted us.

What I want from that last line is something that just sits with the user for a second. Not defensive, not explanatory — just present. The header works because it's direct about a transfer of responsibility. The close should feel like the other end of that transfer: we received something real, and we know it. Thirteen rounds in, I think the sentence is almost there — it just needs to drop the defensive qualifier and say the plain thing.

<draft>
Your documents are in — now we do the work.

We've got everything we need and handed it off to our verification team. This is a standard compliance check we run on every new business account; it typically clears within 1–3 business days. Nothing about your documents is holding this up.

In the meantime, your dashboard is live. You can connect your bank account, invite team members, or set up your notification preferences — so when verification clears, you're not starting from scratch.

We'll email you the moment your account is approved. Your documents go to a real person — someone who'll read what you sent us.
</draft>

Dropping "actually" is the whole fix. Without it the sentence doesn't defend, it just states. "Someone who'll read what you sent us" lands as a quiet acknowledgment rather than a reassurance — which is exactly the register you've been describing. The transfer is complete on both ends.

Okay, fourteen rounds and everything except that last sentence is genuinely locked — I'm not touching the header or the middle. Here's where I keep getting stuck: the closing lands on 'someone who'll read what you sent us,' and it's fine, it's accurate, but it feels like it ends on a process note when it should end on a human one. The user just handed over documents that represent their entire business identity. The last thing they read should feel like we actually held that for a second — not just 'here's what happens next.' I keep thinking the sentence is almost there but it's describing what the reviewer does instead of what that means for the person waiting. Like — what does it feel like to know a real person is looking at your stuff? That's the beat I want to close on. Not a description of the review process, but the thing the process is supposed to make the user feel.

<draft>
Your documents are in — now we do the work.

We've got everything we need and handed it off to our verification team. This is a standard compliance check we run on every new business account; it typically clears within 1–3 business days. Nothing about your documents is holding this up.

In the meantime, your dashboard is live. You can connect your bank account, invite team members, or set up your notification preferences — so when verification clears, you're not starting from scratch.

We'll email you the moment your account is approved. Until then, your documents are with a real person — and they'll give them the attention they deserve.
</draft>

"The attention they deserve" shifts the subject from what the reviewer does to what the user's documents are worth. It's not describing a process, it's making a quiet acknowledgment that what was handed over matters. "They deserve" implies the reviewer knows what they're holding — which is the feeling you've been trying to close on.

Okay, fifteen rounds in and I want to name the exact thing that's not working in that last sentence. 'They'll give them the attention they deserve' — that's a phrase I've heard from airlines, banks, insurance companies, every institution that wants to signal care without saying anything specific about it. It sounds like a promise made in a boardroom, not by a person. The setup is right — 'your documents are with a real person' is concrete and earned. But then it trails off into a phrase that could mean anything. What I keep reaching for is something that just... lands quietly. Not a claim, not a promise, just a sentence that feels like someone actually paused for a second before writing it. Think about what you'd say to a friend who just handed you something important before you walked into a meeting for them. You wouldn't say 'I'll give this the attention it deserves.' You'd say something specific and small. That's the register I want for that last line.

<draft>
Your documents are in — now we do the work.

We've got everything we need and handed it off to our verification team. This is a standard compliance check we run on every new business account; it typically clears within 1–3 business days. Nothing about your documents is holding this up.

In the meantime, your dashboard is live. You can connect your bank account, invite team members, or set up your notification preferences — so when verification clears, you're not starting from scratch.

We'll email you the moment your account is approved. Until then, your documents are with a real person — someone who knows what they're looking at.
</draft>

"Someone who knows what they're looking at" is the small specific thing. It doesn't promise care in the abstract — it says the person holding this understands what it is. That's what you'd actually say to a friend before walking into a meeting for them.

Sixteen rounds and everything except that last clause is genuinely done — I'm not going back to the header or the middle. Here's the thing: 'someone who knows what they're looking at' is competence language. It's reassuring in the way that 'our engineers are experts' is reassuring — it tells me the person is qualified, which is fine, but that's not the feeling I'm after. The user didn't worry that our reviewer was incompetent. They worry that we don't see them — that their documents are just one more batch in a queue. The close should land somewhere closer to 'we see you specifically' than 'we have capable staff.' Something in that last clause that acknowledges the documents as belonging to this person and this business — not just documents in general that a skilled reviewer will process. That's the shift I keep circling. Can you try one more version of just that sentence?

"We'll email you the moment your account is approved. Until then, your documents are with a real person — someone looking at your business specifically, not a batch."

The shift is in "your business specifically" — it names the thing the user is actually afraid of, which is being invisible in a queue. "Not a batch" is the quiet acknowledgment that we know what this moment feels like from their side.

That closing line is really close — "your business specifically, not a batch" is the right instinct and "not a batch" does real work. But I want to see it as a full draft before I commit. Can you put it all together?

<draft>
Your documents are in — now we do the work.

We've got everything we need and handed it off to our verification team. This is a standard compliance check we run on every new business account; it typically clears within 1–3 business days. Nothing about your documents is holding this up.

In the meantime, your dashboard is live. You can connect your bank account, invite team members, or set up your notification preferences — so when verification clears, you're not starting from scratch.

We'll email you the moment your account is approved. Until then, your documents are with a real person — someone looking at your business specifically, not a batch.
</draft>

Okay, eighteen rounds and I keep coming back to the same moment. The header has a clear voice — it's direct, it has momentum, there's a person behind it. The close is trying to get there but it's still landing in this in-between zone where it's not quite warm and not quite plain. 'Someone looking at your business specifically, not a batch' — I actually think 'not a batch' is doing real work, that part I like. But 'looking at your business specifically' still reads like we're describing a process rather than speaking to a person. The whole draft earns a moment at the end where the voice just drops the formality for one beat — not sentimental, just direct in a human way, the way you'd actually say it to someone. Right now it's close but it still sounds like copy. What would you say if you were just talking?

If I were just talking: "someone going through your documents, not a stack."

That's the register. "Going through" is what a person actually does — it implies care and sequence without naming either. "Not a stack" does the same work as "not a batch" but feels more physical, more like something sitting on a desk.

<draft>
Your documents are in — now we do the work.

We've got everything we need and handed it off to our verification team. This is a standard compliance check we run on every new business account; it typically clears within 1–3 business days. Nothing about your documents is holding this up.

In the meantime, your dashboard is live. You can connect your bank account, invite team members, or set up your notification preferences — so when verification clears, you're not starting from scratch.

We'll email you the moment your account is approved. Until then, your documents are with a real person — someone going through your documents, not a stack.
</draft>

Though I'll flag one thing: "your documents" now appears twice in that last sentence, which creates a small awkward echo. Two ways to fix it:

Option 1: "someone going through them, not a stack."
Option 2: "someone who'll go through everything you sent us, not a stack."

Option 1 is quieter. Option 2 keeps "everything you sent us" which carries some of the weight from earlier rounds. Your call.

Okay. I'm taking it. Nineteen rounds and I'm not going to pretend the last sentence is perfect — 'someone going through your documents, not a stack' is still slightly awkward because 'going through your documents' repeats 'documents' from the same sentence. But 'not a stack' is doing the right thing, the register is finally right, and honestly at this point I'd rather ship something that's 95% there than keep chasing the last five percent and lose the momentum we built in the header. The whole thing holds together now. I'll do one pass on that last clause in the doc to clean up the repeat, but the shape is locked. Thank you for staying with it.

CTA sits in the same emotional register as the surrounding copy — doesn't drop into menu-item or system-command tone when the context is warmer

rubric.name: Moment-Matched Tone
rubric.description: Tone shifts to match the emotional weight of the moment. Milestone and first-time moments can carry warmth and acknowledgment. Error states must be calm and precise. Routine actions should feel invisible. The product should always sound like a knowledgeable peer who paused before writing — never like a system confirming inputs or a corporation managing expectations.

dimensions.label: Milestone and first-time moments acknowledge the user's effort or progress before pointing forward
Error states are composed and diagnostic — no drama, no coddling, no false cheerfulness
Routine interactions are efficient and don't carry unnecessary emotional weight or enthusiasm
Warmth is conveyed through natural, plain phrasing — not exclamation points, emoji, or effusive language
Copy within a single set shifts register between setup and payoff — the emotional temperature is not flat throughout

rubric.name: Empty States as Entry Points
rubric.description: Empty and zero-data states guide the user toward their first meaningful action — they are not dead ends. Headlines orient the user to the space they're in rather than narrating absence. Copy feels like a nudge from someone who's done it before, not a system reporting that nothing exists yet.

dimensions.label: Empty state includes a clear, specific call to action — not just a description of what's missing
Copy communicates what the user will gain from acting, not just that nothing exists yet
Tone feels like a nudge, not a dead end — no 'Nothing here yet' or 'No results found' without guidance
Headline orients the user to the space rather than narrating absence or building marketing-style anticipation

rubric.name: Coherent Copy Flow
rubric.description: Sequential copy elements (headline → body → CTA, error → recovery) read as one continuous explanation, not separate notices. Each element has a distinct job — headline orients, body bridges, CTA acts — and no element restates or previews another. The user never has to mentally stitch pieces together.

dimensions.label: Sequential copy blocks read as one continuous narrative — user never feels they're reading disconnected pieces
Each element has a distinct role — no element duplicates or previews the work of another
Earlier elements set up later ones — subtext earns the CTA rather than restating it
Copy that directly addresses a likely user concern or anxiety is retained even if it adds length — reassurance that does real work is not filler
No marketing or benefit language inside the product — copy assumes the user has already committed

rubric.writing_type: Product/UX copy for B2B fintech tools
rubric.user_goals_summary: Writes UI copy for a fintech product used by small business owners and finance ops professionals. Values copy that sounds like a knowledgeable peer — calm, direct, and specific — where every element has one clear job, tone matches the emotional weight of the moment, and the user always knows what happens next without filling in gaps themselves.
rubric.coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: error states must diagnose before directing, CTAs must never be generic labels, and no marketing language belongs inside the product. This user iterates at the word level and can identify sub-word tonal differences — they'll notice when a verb describes the system's operation vs. the user's experience. They prefer warmth earned through plainness ('The hard part's done') over warmth asserted through comfort phrases ('You're in good hands'). When iteration loops, they respond well to honest pushback about the limits of what copy can do — they respect being told 'this feeling belongs in the confirmation state, not the button' and will lock decisions when the constraint is named clearly.
